speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two with survivor Helen Greenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Helen, what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to recall, before the war yet, an instant that my father went with my mom for a walk because they fixed the bridge in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean-- excuse me. I'm so used with New York, because I lived there. In Warsaw, they fixed the bridge and they took a walk. And they probably talked Yiddish-- Jewish. And a young fellow came by and was hitting my father. And my father was calling out for police. He ran away, a policeman came by, and he said, what's happening? He says, well, a-- a young punk, so to say, attacked me. And he says, well, I don't see any blood. Well, that was a bad experience. By then, my brother that was in New York-- my middle brother--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leo. We lost- we-- we wrote to him about it. And he was flabbergasted, because there's a lot of racism in this country, but, I mean, some of it is hidden. At that-- those years, it was not as obvious as it is now with the racism and bigotry. Because I talk to the kids in school, and I talk to them, and I tell them to be aware of the racism and bigotry. Because it's ruining countries. Because when-- when the Nazis were bombarding the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the countries, they ruined half of it. Then when the Allies came-- so it took decades for the countries to rebuild themselves. That's why I can't understand that those racists and bigots don't realize what they are doing to their countries.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father ever think about going to Israel or going to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We had-- he bought land in Israel, but I never knew where he bought it. My oldest brother tried to find out when he moved to Israel, but he couldn't find out. But my middle brother wanted to take us to-- bring us to-- to the United States. But my father said, I don't know the language. Here I'm a well-to-do person, and there I'll be a stranger-- like to say, a stranger in paradise. And I have to start from the beginning. And I'm not a youngster anymore. So that's why we stayed. Even when they started in the small cities-- small towns-- to put the armbands with the Star of David on, he said, oh, they are not going to do that in Warsaw. He was so proud of Warsaw. He thought, you know, Warsaw is supposed to be a haven. But it was not.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you begin to wear a star in Warsaw-- Jewish star?

speaker: interviewee
text: First they started 19-- I think it was '41 they started with the War-- with the wearing the armbands. And '42-- don't ask me months, because, like I told you--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Block. And at that time-- till then-- at that time, they took the supers out. And they made Jewish people as supers for the buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in a building?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in a building that we were-- it was like a double-- one yard, then you went into the next yard, and we were like 80 some odd apartments.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish people. And in War-- in Poland, you were registered, you know? Everybody knew who lives there. So-- but I encountered a-- mom and I one time went a few streets away to bring something, because we still had a little more food because-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to bring something to a friend of hers that was sick. And all of sudden, going home-- they used to close up blocks on each side. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and to every entrance to the building, somebody stood there so people couldn't run away, and they took all the people from the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say somebody, who was the person?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was either Jewish policeman-- at that time, we had Jewish policeman-- or [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nazis. I'm so careful not to say Germans. And you couldn't run in, and they grabbed all the people from the street on the-- on the trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, lo and behold. We look around, and there's one place-- one-- one entrance to the building that nobody was there. We ran in. We ran into the super-- at that time, was a Jewish super. And I said, will you hide us? He said, I cannot do that. But go upstairs-- all the way upstairs. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And from the attic, there is a thing to-- an opening to go out on the roof. Can you imagine my mom, being in her late '50s at that time, to go up four flights, go up into the attic of the building and climb out on the roof and hiding behind the chimney? And that's the God's truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, my family at that time-- my father was still, my sister, my brother-in-law-- they all thought that we are goners. Because they heard about that block and they knew that we had to cross that street. And then the super eventually came up to tell us that they are gone. We came home, and we quickly, quickly-- we made a package of certain things, foods and things, and brought it to that super. That's the kind of people my parents were, always thanking people. But also they started to-- young kids, before they closed up to ghetto, some of the big companies that were giving out certain things, like there used to be a company-- chocolate company, [? Weddell, ?] so they were giving out, like, a package with different chocolates for a zloty, a dollar.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister was in there, and my sister never looked like, you know, Jewish. Her eyes were the blue eyes and all. But they had a sixth sense-- kids-- I'm talking about kids-- a sixth sense. They were standing in line and showing to the soldiers. They-- These were the Polish kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish kids. Pointing out. They learned the word Jude, which means Jew. The rest was in Polish-- pointing out [NON-ENGLISH], to take them out from the lines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the ghetto itself. When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it begin for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. That's when I told-- it was depressing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We still were-- we were still in our-- in our-- it just happened that our-- our building was still-- in the big ghetto, our building was still there. So we were still home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone else come and live in your apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We shared with neighbors. Nobody came. The only one that a friend of my mom's came up one time when my mother cooked a pot of soup, and she dealed it out. And she always used to-- she dealt out the soup. And then my mom had a saying-- a little thin soup with a porcelain plate and a silver spoon. Because we didn't know about everyday dishes or everyday silverware. We ate every day from the same things, besides Passover. She had the saying always, the hands that are putting away the Passover dishes should live to see the next Passover, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you say that in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know something? I always bring in English words. It became so--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Pesach.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] Pesach. [INAUDIBLE], well. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So a woman-- a knock on the door, and a woman comes in, acquainted of my mother's. She was-- already you could see swelling. And-- and she said, oh, you just in time. She quickly took her plate, her-- with soup, went in, and quickly reheated it, you know, and brought it. And mom didn't have any soup at that time, because she shared it with that woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the food to eat from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we got ration cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who provided that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, ration cards-- they were giving food. You paid, and that was the list. It was very little in the ration cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who gave you the ration cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Ger-- the-- the Nazis were giving ration cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you so careful about not saying Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It's just, like, one Jew does something, and they say, the goddamn Jews. And that really brings something out in me that I cannot do.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you see the Germans differently.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I know a lot of Germans. And, sorry to say, that a lot of Germans helped more hiding Jews than-- than Polish people. They-- they gave out hiding places from whole families for nothing, or for a bottle of vodka-- sorry to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about the ghetto. How long did you remain in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God, I don't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we still had some food that we got hold of before they closed the ghetto, so we had some that we could really eat decent. Meat, out of the question. And so we-- you just lived on-- [LAUGHS] so we became people that lived just on dairy, or so to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about school? Were you able--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. School was finished when they first walked in when I was 16 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do the whole day in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was reading. Mostly I was reading books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any kind of social life for young people your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no social life. Not for me, social life. I was-- like I said to you, I-- until this day, my son knows-- he's so careful to say things, because I'm a very sensitive person. I can-- I can get very depressed about things. So I-- it started, maybe, during to war. So-- and then I took over, like I said, because I was always a nut when it came to cleanliness. So I took over with cleaning. And even until now, it's not an old lady's thing about keeping clean. It's just with me, when we came to Israel in 1972, the family that was there told my husband, oh, your wife, when she was a young girl, already the maid was not allowed to go through her drawers. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain-- until what time did you remain in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- in the ghetto-- then they started to close-- made a smaller ghetto. So my mom and I, we went to see if we could get things together and go to the Umshlagplatz, and-- because they promised you, you know, they're going to take you to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of place that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Umshlagplatz was the gathering place in Warsaw to-- to take-- to take the people out. They were the-- the cattle wagons that they were taking out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it located?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- on the-- in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember where, what area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not get there. I'll tell you what happened. So we met a man that was very friendly with us. And he said, where you going? I said, well, we are going to get ready and we'll go to the Umshlagplatz.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, they had the smaller ghetto. And he said, wait a minute. Don't go. I can arrange something for you to go in to [? Schulze's ?] uniform factory, the two of you. We have to bribe-- well, we had jewlry, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: We can bribe with some jewelry the-- the guy that [? isn't-- ?] and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we'll do it. So we wound up working at [? Schulze's ?] uniform factory in the small ghetto. Every few days, they had selections.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the rest of your mother's children? Where were they, your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, excuse me. Excuse me. My sister and her husband, on the outskirts of Warsaw, which is called [? Progust-- ?] old, old city, they had two Wehrmacht camps, which were not Nazis, really. So they were-- they had two camps. My-- my brother wound up in one of those camps, which was not bad, and my sister and her husband wound up in the other one. They were not too far away from one another. And my mom and I wound up at [? Schulze's ?] factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the factory. We-- we lived, you know, several people in-- gathered into-- in rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was gone by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took him when-- before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, I forgot [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And every few days, they had selections. So one day, they had a selection and they took my mom. And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the selection?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Jewish community in any way involved in the selections?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They stood there, and just having the whim of who they liked, whose face they liked and whose face they didn't like. As a matter of fact, I used to wear on my neck a little bag made out of--

speaker: interviewer
text: You have to move your hand from the microphone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, excuse me-- a little bag made out of material where I kept the jewel-- the family jewelry. And at that time when we were five that they took-- they-- we stood there for the selections. And that man that took care of us to get the job there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A man that we knew from--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, God. I don't remember. His first name was [? Heinich ?] or something like that. And his sister was among the five. My mom was there. So, like, two of us went to the one side, and the others, and his sister wound up in the bad side.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, look, I'm going to give you some jewelry. Go to Umschlagplatz. See if you can bribe somebody to get them out. He came out with the jewelry. He returned it to me, and he said, I couldn't do a damn thing. What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I was by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took her away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where she was taken to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then I found out-- when they were showing in the '70s the show about the-- about the-- the war, and then I noticed my mom in one of those pictures. And that's when I went to New York. I found out where NBC borrowed the-- these were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pictures that they took, the Nazis, to show one day, there were some people that existed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where was she killed, which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one of the camps. I don't know. It shows only a picture. She's surrounded with other-- with other women, with dead bodies, and soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your mother when she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: When she was taken away, she was in her late 50s. My father was, like, four years older than my mom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you next, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So when my brother used to come into the ghetto, because where he was working in that Wehrmacht camp, they had, like, different things there that the factories were making to-- to-- for-- for the soldiers to come and pick up the-- whatever staple was available.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- one time he came into the ghetto, and then he found out my mom was gone. So he talked to the commandant, which is the head of the whatever, if he could bring his sister in. And that's how I wound up with my brother in that camp. What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where my sis-- where my sister was, it was called Kol-- [? Koirenlage. ?] And-- and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was in the other one. It was-- give me a minute. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Oh, God. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I talk about it all the time, right? I'm so nervous now that I--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. If you remember, you'll tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the Wehrmacht. There were no SS or anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were stapling the staples on-- on-- on the shelves, whatever arrived, for when they were coming out, the-- the trucks to pick certain things up. So it was lined up for them. Then we found out that the lag-- the camp is going to be closed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my brother and-- oh, excuse me. My brother tried to get, from the other camp-- excuse me-- my sister. Excuse me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] My sister and her husband, so we were all together, the four of us. And that was the youngest from the three brothers. What were their names? What were the boys' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- [? Edic, ?] the one-- the youngest, and my sister was [? Rosie, ?] and her husband was Abraham [? Zwichel. ?] So we were all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, when we kind of thought-- they were kind of telling that they were going to close it up, so my brother and my brother-in-law, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when they went the next time to, you know, pick things for the camp, they were going to look for hiding places.

speaker: interviewee
text: While they were away, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they put us on trucks and took us into Warsaw, to the ghetto, to-- it wasn't really ghetto. Yes, whatever. It-- the one-- away from people. Meanwhile, I don't know how my brother and my brother-in-law found out that we were loaded on trucks already. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'll try and be as calm as possible. Because the last time I saw my youngest brother is running after the truck. He wanted to jump on the truck. That was the last time I saw him. After the war, I found out-- we had a friend. He spoke more-- better Jewish than I did. Because I'm the youngest, so mostly, I spoke Polish. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That he was hiding with him and his wife and children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sure enough, somebody squealed. They took out the whole family and my brother, and they shot them in the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your brother when he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see now. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Either late 20s or early 30s. Good looking guy. I showed you his picture. As a matter of fact, they wanted to make an actor out of him in the Jewish theater.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then-- so they took us another night into the-- into the Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it used to be, before the war, they used to have a jail there. So they kept us overnight there. And from-- Was this in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Warsaw. And from there, they took us to Lublin. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was just for a short time where we were, like, digging ditches or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was with my sister. Now, then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many other people you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. All I was concerned about my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these just women or men and women?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, men and women. So they took us to that place. And then when the Warsaw uprising started, and they started to bring some of them over there--

speaker: interviewer
text: To Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Lublin-- and we wound up all with them together into Majdanek. That was the first death camp. By then, we knew already that there is such thing as showers where you don't get water, but gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: People that did try to run-- to escape, and people started to know what's happening. Where it's supposed to be at every of those death camps, they had a sign, Arbeit macht frei, meaning work makes free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we undre-- first we had to undress in front of SS men. Mind you, SS men. Stark naked, walking by, to go in into the shower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we knew we were going for the sure death. But we didn't. We went into a shower. And then we got some clothes that was full of lice. What did you say to sister? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, that's it. That's it. We are done. But when we came out, we have to march again in front of the-- the-- the SS men. And then they decided who is going-- no, excuse me. They decided before we went to the shower who is going left or right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by the time we came out, we were getting different clothes, shoes that didn't match, and-- Was your hair shaved at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not-- I never had my hair shaved. And would you believe that I had blonde curls once upon a time? And this is my true color. I'm bragging about it. But-- so we came out, and we were taken to the barracks. We wound up with three more girls that we-- because each cot, whatever you call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: The bunk bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bunk beds were taking five people at the time. And we were five people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three other girls, which we met and became friendly-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What country were they from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were also mostly from Warsaw. So it was a good thing, because we had a lot in common. And we were there, I don't know how long, a few months. And all of a sudden, they said, they are taking people out to a different camp. I said, Rosie, you know something-- and the three girls-- no matter where we go out of a death camp, there is a gas chamber. There is a-- there is a ovens. Let's see what we wind up with.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of job had you done in Majdanek?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Majdanek, we were working mostly in the fields, on all four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Weeding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Because you know, when you put somebody in you-- and try to-- I have to close the door.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I can hear his-- does it bother? No. So this-- so the weeding-- just to torture us. There was nothing, really. Carrying all kinds of heavy, heavy boulders from one place to the other, and the next day, from there to another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see people taken to the gas chambers, to the ovens? That's OK. Someone's knocking at the door. That's what she hears. [INAUDIBLE] OK, let's stop here with tape two then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Please, let me--

speaker: interviewer
text: One second. Just disconnect the mic--
